Issues.Title: Implement RFC 9931 ┬¦8 for HTTP/1.1 CONNECT in Kestrel
Issues.Body: Per [RFC 9931 ┬¦8](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9931#section-8), an HTTP/1.1 server that rejects a `CONNECT` request must close the connection. Kestrel currently keeps it open. This change disables keep-alive when the method is `CONNECT`, the version is HTTP/1.1, and the final status is Ōēź 300. HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and accepted (2xx) CONNECTs are unaffected.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Implement RFC 9931 ┬¦8 for HTTP/1.1 CONNECT in Kestrel" (#67929)

Issues.Title: Fix url normalizer backslashes
Issues.Body:
# Fix url normalizer backslashes

UrlNormalizer.CollapseLeadingSlashes (introduced in #66961) was intended to prevent rewrite/redirect targets from resolving as scheme-relative URLs (//host, /\host). However, it only triggered when the string already started with /

## Description

`CollapseLeadingSlashes` now treats a leading `\` the same as a leading `/` for the purposes of triggering normalization, closing the bypass regardless of which character (or combination of `/` and `\`) starts the string:

```csharp
if ((url[0] != '/' && url[0] != '\\') ||
    (url[0] == '/' && (url.Length == 1 || (url[1] != '/' && url[1] != '\\'))))
{
    return url;
}

var firstNonSlash = url.AsSpan().IndexOfAnyExcept('/', '\\');
if (firstNonSlash < 0)
{
    return "/";
}
return string.Concat("/", url.AsSpan(firstNonSlash));
```

The fast-path condition returns early only when:

there's no leading `/` or `\` at all, or
the string already starts with exactly one / (i.e. it's already normalized).

Any other case, including a bare leading `\`, or multiple leading slashes/backslashes in any combination falls through to the collapsing logic.

Why a hybrid (if-path + IndexOfAnyExcept) approach instead of a plain scalar loop

The original implementation used a simple manual scan with a `while` loop checking `url[i] == '/' || url[i] == '\\'` one character at a time. I benchmarked three approaches

A pure if-based scalar scan (closest to the original code, extended to also catch `\`). 
A pure `Span<char>.IndexOfAnyExcept('/', '\\')` call with no fast path.
The hybrid version above: fast-path checks for the 0/1-leading-slash case, falling back to `IndexOfAnyExcept` otherwise.

Results (ns/op, representative rows):

| Input | Scalar `if` | Pure `IndexOfAnyExcept` | Hybrid |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `""` / `already normalized` / | ~0.2ŌĆō0.5 ns | ~0.06ŌĆō0.2 ns | ~0.07ŌĆō0.3 ns |
| `///` (short run) | ~2.9 ns | ~4.1 ns | ~3.1 ns |
| Long string, long leading slash run (54 chars) | ~40.2 ns | ~12.5 ns | ~13.2 ns |
| `/////site.com/path` | ~12.1 ns | ~13.0 ns | ~13.5 ns |

Given that this method sits on the rewrite/redirect request path and its inputs are influenced by external clients, I prioritized keeping the common case cheap while making the worst case resilient rather than linear.

Fixes #67812
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Fix url normalizer backslashes" (#67928)

Issues.Title: Support for serializing/deserializing `float[]` as `Float32Array` via Blazor JSInterop
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem.

When you work with streams of analog data like sound data, you often use float arrays for storing data. Currently, if you work with float arrays (`float[]`) in .NET, but want to transfer this array to JavaScript to use some browser API, we have to convert the array to a byte array (`byte[]`) to efficiently transfer it as only byte arrays are optimized for Blazor JSInterop. So you end up with something like this every time you want to transfer a float array.
- Copy a float array into a byte array (using something like `Buffer.BlockCopy`).
- Send the byte array to JS via JSInterop where it will become an `Uint8Array`.
- Get the `ArrayBuffer` (memory view) of the `Uint8Array`.
- Create a `Float32Array` from the `ArrayBuffer`.

That is a rather tedious process especially because you will have to write your own JS function for the JS part (unless you use a framework like [Blazor.WebIDL](https://github.com/KristofferStrube/Blazor.WebIDL))



### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like for Blazor JSInterop to support efficient serialization of `float[]`s to the JS `Float32Array` type so that we can make calls like the one below:
```csharp
IJSObjectReference audioBuffer; // Some audio buffer
float[] source = [1f, 0f, -1f, 0f, 1f, 0f, -1f, 0f];
ulong channelNumber = 0;
var jsFloatArray = await audioBuffer.InvokeVoidAsync("copyToChannel", source, channelNumber) 
```
And I would likewise like for Blazor JSInterop to support efficient deserialization of `float[]`s from `Float32Array`s so that we can make calls like the one below:
```csharp
IJSObjectReference audioBuffer; // Some audio buffer
ulong channel = 0;
float[] data = await audioBuffer.InvokeAsync<float[]>("getChannelData", channel);
```

### Additional context

This wish was first reported in `Blazor.WebIDL` and would be useful in `Blazor.WebAudio` where I have also had the same need previously. https://github.com/KristofferStrube/Blazor.WebIDL/issues/42

In the linked issue I have shown some alternative solutions for how you can achieve the desired transfer today, but I think the proposed feature in this issue would be the best solution.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Support for serializing/deserializing `float[]` as `Float32Array` via Blazor JSInterop" (#67926)

Issues.Title: fix: honor OpenAPI transformer registration order
Issues.Body:
Fixes the OpenAPI transformer ordering issue discussed in https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/65723#issuecomment-5025789850.

This keeps the existing per-transformer-type lists while tracking registrations in a unified internal list so document transformers registered before operation/schema transformers can run before operation generation. This allows document transformers to pre-populate document-level tag metadata without operation tag discovery replacing it.

Validation:
- Docker: dotnet test src/OpenApi/test/Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi.Tests/Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi.Tests.csproj --filter 'FullyQualifiedName~DocumentTransformerTests|FullyQualifiedName~OperationTransformerTests'
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "fix: honor OpenAPI transformer registration order" (#67925)

Issues.Title: [DirectTls] Add metrics, tracing, and logging on par with the Sockets transport and runtime epoll engine
Issues.Body:
Follow-up for the experimental **DirectTls** transport (proposal #67909, draft implementation #67912). DirectTls currently ships with almost no telemetry. Because it terminates TLS **inside the transport** (on the `TlsEventPump` epoll threads) rather than through `SslStream` in `HttpsConnectionMiddleware`, it both **loses existing observability** and adds a new, unobserved execution surface (the pump). This issue tracks bringing metrics / tracing / logging up to parity with what the **Sockets transport** and the **runtime's epoll socket engine** already provide. ## Motivation / gaps ### 1. TLS handshake metrics are silently lost `kestrel.tls_handshake.duration` (Histogram) and `kestrel.active_tls_handshakes` (UpDownCounter), on Meter `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel`, are emitted **only** from `HttpsConnectionMiddleware` (`_metrics.TlsHandshakeStart/Stop(...)`). DirectTls makes that middleware a no-op ŌĆö the handshake happens on the pump thread against `TlsSocketSession`. So for DirectTls endpoints these two metrics are **never recorded**. That's the wrong thing to lose for a transport whose entire purpose is TLS performance: handshake latency and in-flight handshake count are exactly what operators need to see. The pump must record the same instruments at handshake begin / complete / fail (including `ConnectionEndReason.TlsHandshakeFailed` on failures), reusing `KestrelMetrics` so the data lands on the existing meter/tags and dashboards keep working. > Note: transport-agnostic connection metrics (`kestrel.active_connections`, `kestrel.connection.duration`, `kestrel.rejected_connections`, `kestrel.queued_connections`, `kestrel.queued_requests`) are recorded in Kestrel core's connection pipeline and **continue to work** for DirectTls ŌĆö no action needed there. The gap is specifically the TLS-handshake instruments above. ### 2. Connection lifecycle tracing, including backpressure The Sockets transport has a full `LoggerMessage`-based trace surface in `SocketsLog.cs` (category `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Transport.Sockets`) that DirectTls has no equivalent for: | Event (Sockets `SocketsLog`) | Meaning | DirectTls equivalent to add | | --- | --- | --- | | `ConnectionPause` / `ConnectionResume` | **backpressure** ŌĆö input pipe hit `PauseWriterThreshold` / recovered | receive loop pauses/resumes the fd (deregister/re-arm `EPOLLIN`) when `Application.Output` flush backpressures | | `ConnectionReadFin` | peer half-close (read FIN) | `SSL_read` / `TlsSocketSession` reports EOF | | `ConnectionWriteFin` / `ConnectionWriteRst` | graceful vs abortive close | send loop / shutdown path | | `ConnectionError` / `ConnectionReset` | I/O error / RST | pump + `ConnectionIoState` error paths | **Backpressure is the one we care about most.** In DirectTls the receive loop decrypts on the pump thread and flushes into `Application.Output`; when the app/Kestrel is slow the pipe's `PauseWriterThreshold` trips and the flush stops completing. The pump must then stop reading that fd (drop `EPOLLIN`) and re-arm when the reader drains ŌĆö and we need to *see* that happen (how often, how long connections sit paused), just like `ConnectionPause`/`ConnectionResume` do for sockets. Without it, a stalled or backpressured pump is invisible. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Pump as epoll pump thread participant Pipe as Application.Output pipe participant App as Kestrel / app Pump->>Pipe: FlushAsync decrypted bytes Note over Pipe: PauseWriterThreshold reached, flush does not complete Pipe-->>Pump: backpressure - flush pending Note over Pump: stop reading fd, drop EPOLLIN
log ConnectionPause, bump paused-connections gauge App->>Pipe: reads and drains Pipe-->>Pump: ResumeWriterThreshold, flush completes Note over Pump: re-arm EPOLLIN
log ConnectionResume, clear gauge ``` ### 3. Pump / engine health telemetry (inspired by the runtime epoll engine) The runtime's `SocketAsyncEngine` (Linux epoll) is the direct analogue of our `TlsEventPump` ŌĆö a small fixed set of dedicated epoll threads pulling readiness events ŌĆö and `System.Net.Sockets` (`SocketsTelemetry` EventSource) exposes engine-level counters (connections established, bytes sent/received, etc.). We have zero visibility into the pump. We should add engine-level telemetry, e.g.: - connections currently owned per pump / total, accepts per second - connections currently paused for backpressure (gauge, ties to ┬¦2) - bytes read / written through the pump - optional: `epoll_wait` iteration/wakeup counts, ready-event batch sizes, pending-op depth These can start as an `EventSource` / `EventCounters` surface (like the runtime's), and graduate the operationally-important ones (paused connections, handshake duration) to `System.Diagnostics.Metrics` instruments.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[DirectTls] Add metrics, tracing, and logging on par with the Sockets transport and runtime epoll engine" (#67916)

Issues.Title: [Experimental] [DirectTls] Support ClientCertificateMode.DelayCertificate (post-handshake client authentication)
Issues.Body:
Follow-up for the experimental **DirectTls** transport (proposal #67909, draft implementation #67912). DirectTls does **not** support `ClientCertificateMode.DelayCertificate` today ŌĆö it throws `NotSupportedException` when that mode is configured. `NoCertificate`, `Allow`, and `Require` are fully supported. ## What DelayCertificate is `ClientCertificateMode.DelayCertificate` lets an app request a client certificate **after** the handshake ŌĆö on demand, the first time the app calls `ITlsConnectionFeature.GetClientCertificateAsync()` (e.g. "only `/admin` needs a client cert; the rest of the site does not"). In TLS 1.3 this is **post-handshake authentication (PHA)** ([RFC 8446 ┬¦4.6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.6.2)): the server sends a `CertificateRequest` after the handshake, and the client answers with a `Certificate` message ŌĆö or an **empty** one to decline. This is an **HTTP/1.1-only** feature: HTTP/2 prohibits TLS renegotiation / PHA ([RFC 8740 ┬¦2.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8740#section-2.1)). ## Why SslStream can do it and DirectTls cannot The difference is **who owns the transport**. - **`SslStream`** owns the socket and drives the PHA exchange frame-by-frame, watching the crypto status transition `ContinueNeeded ŌåÆ OK`. When the client declines (empty Certificate), it still processes every frame itself, so it *knows* the exchange finished and can surface `RemoteCertificateNotAvailable`. - **DirectTls** hands the raw fd to OpenSSL and reads through the runtime's `TlsSocketSession.Read`. The PHA flight is processed **inside** the runtime's socket read and then silently swallowed. The public `Read()` only ever returns `Complete` (real app data) / `Closed` / `NeedMoreData`. There is **no return value, event, or status** that says "a post-handshake Certificate just arrived." ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as Kestrel app participant T as DirectTls transport participant S as TlsSocketSession Read - runtime participant Peer as Client App->>T: GetClientCertificateAsync T->>Peer: server post-handshake CertificateRequest Peer-->>S: Certificate message, or EMPTY to decline Note over S: runtime decrypts and processes the PHA flight,
populates cert, then continues and swallows it S-->>T: Read returns Complete app-data or NeedMoreData Note over T: transport never sees the transition.
Success: poll GetRemoteCertificate, non-null.
Decline: cert stays null, indistinguishable from still waiting ``` ## The core gap | | Success (client sent a cert) | Decline (client sent empty Certificate) | | --- | --- | --- | | How DirectTls could detect it | poll `GetRemoteCertificate()` until non-null | **no signal** ŌĆö cert stays null, exactly like "still waiting" | So **success is pollable but decline is not observable at all.** The only way to resolve a decline on the fd path today is a **forced timeout** (wait N seconds; if the cert is still null, treat as declined) ŌĆö which couples correctness to a magic delay and adds latency to exactly the requests that declined. That's not acceptable, so the mode is deferred. ## What unblocks it A minimal, `Read`-decoupled signal from the runtime that a post-handshake Certificate flight completed. With it, DirectTls resolves **both** success and decline deterministically, with no timeout.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Experimental] [DirectTls] Support ClientCertificateMode.DelayCertificate (post-handshake client authentication)" (#67915)

Issues.Title: [Experimental] [Perf] Restore accept4() single-syscall accept path for the DirectTls transport (Connection: close)
Issues.Body:
_Follow-up perf work for the experimental **DirectTls** transport (proposal #67909, draft implementation #67912)._ _The goal was to make the **first** PR as small and reviewable as possible. Therefore this is a separate issue as follow-up improvement._ The initial DirectTls PR deliberately keeps the connection **accept** path on the managed `Socket` API so that **epoll is the transport's only native dependency**. This issue tracks reintroducing a single-syscall accept via Linux [`accept4(2)`](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/accept4.2.html) as an **isolated, separately-benchmarked** optimization. In measurements it was worth **~4%** throughput on the `Connection: close` (one-request-per-connection) scenario, which is the priority workload for this transport. ## Why the accept path matters here The accept loop runs **once per connection**. For keep-alive / many-requests-per-connection workloads, accept cost is amortized to near-zero and invisible. But the priority scenario for DirectTls is the opposite ŌĆö **`Connection: close`, one request per connection** ŌĆö so every request pays the full accept + socket-setup cost. That is exactly where shaving accept syscalls becomes measurable, and where we observed **~4%**. ## What the current managed accept path costs ```csharp // TlsEventPump.AcceptConnections() today accepted = _listenSocket!.Accept(); // managed Socket allocation + accept accepted.Blocking = false; // fcntl(F_SETFL, O_NONBLOCK) if (_noDelay) accepted.NoDelay = true; // setsockopt(TCP_NODELAY) var remoteEndPoint = accepted.RemoteEndPoint; // getpeername int clientFd = (int)accepted.Handle; ``` Per accepted connection: | Step | Syscall / cost | | --- | --- | | `Socket.Accept()` | `accept4()` **plus** a managed `Socket` allocation + `SafeSocketHandle` setup | | `Blocking = false` | `fcntl(F_GETFL)` + `fcntl(F_SETFL, ŌĆ” \| O_NONBLOCK)` | | `NoDelay = true` | `setsockopt(IPPROTO_TCP, TCP_NODELAY)` | | `RemoteEndPoint` | `getpeername()` + `IPEndPoint` allocation | So ~4ŌĆō5 syscalls plus two managed allocations, every connection. ## What `accept4()` collapses it to `accept4(2)` accepts a connection **and** applies `SOCK_NONBLOCK` **and** returns the peer address in a **single** syscall: ```csharp [LibraryImport(LIBC)] public static unsafe partial int accept4(int sockfd, void* addr, void* addrlen, int flags); public const int SOCK_NONBLOCK = 0x800; // Accept + non-blocking + peer address in one syscall. public static unsafe (int fd, IPEndPoint? remoteEndPoint) AcceptNonBlockingWithPeerAddress(int listenFd) { SockAddrStorage addr = default; int addrLen = sizeof(SockAddrStorage); int clientFd = accept4(listenFd, &addr, &addrLen, SOCK_NONBLOCK); // ...parse sockaddr_in / sockaddr_in6 into an IPEndPoint... } ``` The four-step table collapses to: | Step | Syscall | | --- | --- | | `accept4(fd, &addr, &len, SOCK_NONBLOCK)` | **one** syscall: accept + `O_NONBLOCK` + peer address | | `SetTcpNoDelay(fd)` | `setsockopt(TCP_NODELAY)` (still needed) | `fcntl` (├Ś2) is gone ŌĆö `SOCK_NONBLOCK` in the accept flags does it inline. `getpeername` is gone ŌĆö the address comes back from `accept4`. And there is **no managed `Socket` allocation**: the transport drives the raw `int` fd, wrapping it in a `SafeSocketHandle` only when it hands ownership to the `TlsSocketSession`. Net: ~3 fewer syscalls and 2 fewer allocations per connection. A related accept-path tuning, `TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT` (don't wake the acceptor until data arrives), is in the same bucket and can be reconsidered here too. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph now["Now - managed accept, merged first"] a1["Socket.Accept()"] --> a2["Blocking=false
fcntl x2"] --> a3["NoDelay
setsockopt"] --> a4["RemoteEndPoint
getpeername"] end subgraph later["Later - accept4, deferred perf PR"] b1["accept4 SOCK_NONBLOCK + addr
one syscall"] --> b2["SetTcpNoDelay
setsockopt"] end now -->|"~4% on Connection: close"| later style later fill:#efe ``` ## Implementation checklist 1. Add the `accept4` P/Invoke + `SOCK_NONBLOCK`, the `SockAddrIn` / `SockAddrIn6` / `SockAddrStorage` structs, and `AcceptNonBlockingWithPeerAddress` to a native interop file. 2. In `TlsEventPump.AcceptConnections`, replace `_listenSocket.Accept()` + `Blocking` / `NoDelay` / `RemoteEndPoint` with `accept4(...)` for the fd + peer address, plus a single `setsockopt` for `TCP_NODELAY`. Wrap the returned `int` fd in an owning `SafeSocketHandle` for the `TlsSocketSession`. 3. Keep the managed path as a **fallback** for non-Linux / when `accept4` is unavailable. 4. Optionally re-add `TCP_DEFER_ACCEPT`. 5. Benchmark **specifically** on `Connection: close` ŌĆö that's where the ~4% lives; a keep-alive rig will show ~0% and hide the win.

Issues.Title: [Experimental] DirectTls transport implementation
Issues.Body:
This PR adds a dedicated, opt-in Kestrel transport that terminates TLS directly on the connection's socket file descriptor via the runtime's low-level `TlsContext` / `TlsSocketSession` API, bypassing `SslStream`. Connection readiness is driven by an `epoll`-based event pump, and the TLS byte stream (`SSL_read` / `SSL_write`) runs on the fd with no managed intermediate copy. The goal is to remove the `SslStream` copy/allocation overhead on the TLS hot path for connection-dense, handshake-heavy workloads. > **Status: experimental.** Gated behind `[Experimental("ASPNETCORE_DIRECTTLS_001")]`. Primarily focused on Linux, where it binds OpenSSL directly to the connection file descriptor and requires OpenSSL (`libssl`) on the host. **Related:** the public API proposal is described at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/67909; the runtime dependency is tracked in https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/128871 (with implementation at [dotnet/runtime#130366](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/130366)). --- ## Usage An app opts a **specific endpoint** into the DirectTls transport; every other endpoint keeps using the default socket transport unchanged. Selection is driven by the endpoint type (`DirectTlsEndpoint`), not by a host-wide transport override. ```csharp builder.WebHost.UseKestrel(); builder.WebHost.UseDirectTlsTransport(); // additive registration builder.WebHost.ConfigureKestrel(options => { var endpoint = new DirectTlsEndpoint(IPAddress.Any, 5001); endpoint.Options.ServerCertificate = certificate; options.Listen(endpoint); // this endpoint is served by DirectTls }); ``` ### Why a dedicated assembly DirectTls ships as its own assembly, `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Server.Kestrel.Transport.DirectTls` (part of the shared framework, `IsAspNetCoreApp=true`), rather than living inside `Transport.Sockets`. Unlike the general-purpose Sockets transport ŌĆö a plaintext, protocol-agnostic byte pipe ŌĆö DirectTls terminates TLS and references `Kestrel.Core` directly to read `HttpProtocols`/`ClientCertificateMode` and to publish `ITlsApplicationProtocolFeature`. Keeping it separate lets it take that `Kestrel.Core` dependency (one-way; core never references the transport) and isolates the experimental runtime dependency (`SYSLIB5007`) and OpenSSL requirement from the default transport. It is `IsTrimmable`, so trimmed/Native AOT apps that never call `UseDirectTlsTransport()` drop it entirely. --- ## Endpoint routing Kestrel already routes endpoints to transports by endpoint type via `IConnectionListenerFactorySelector`. `KestrelServerImpl` stores factories **reversed** (last-registered is evaluated first), and `TransportManager.BindAsync` binds with the first factory whose `CanBind(endpoint)` returns true. The exact `EndPoint` instance passed to `Listen(...)` reaches the factory, so a derived `DirectTlsEndpoint` is preserved (not normalized to a plain `IPEndPoint`). ```mermaid flowchart TD A["options.Listen with a
DirectTlsEndpoint"] --> B{"Endpoint is a
DirectTlsEndpoint?"} B -- yes --> C["DirectTlsTransportFactory
CanBind returns true
tried first after UseKestrel"] C --> D["DirectTls transport
native fd-bound
OpenSSL TLS"] B -- no --> E["Default SocketTransportFactory
CanBind returns true"] E --> F["Default sockets transport
plaintext or SslStream
via UseHttps"] ``` | Endpoint | `DirectTlsTransportFactory.CanBind` | Bound by | |---|---|---| | `DirectTlsEndpoint` (a subtype of `IPEndPoint`) | **true** | DirectTls | | plain `IPEndPoint` / UDS / fd | **false** | default sockets | `DirectTlsTransportFactory.CanBind` returns true **only** for `DirectTlsEndpoint`. Because the two transports live in separate assemblies, routing correctness relies on registration order: `UseDirectTlsTransport()` runs after `UseKestrel()`, so it is last-registered and first-tried, and it claims its endpoints before the default transport sees them. --- ## How it works ### The event pump Each listener owns a pool of `WorkerCount` `epoll` worker threads (`TlsEventPump` / `TlsEventPumpPool`). One transport-level `DirectTlsTransportOptions.WorkerCount` tunes the parallelism. > The `WorkerCount` default is provisional (currently `4`). Before stabilization it should likely track processor count the way the Sockets transport sizes `IOQueueCount` (`Math.Min(ProcessorCount, 16)` on smaller machines, `ProcessorCount / 2` on large ones) rather than a fixed constant ŌĆö this is an open question for review. - **Accept.** Each worker waits for readiness on the shared (`EPOLLEXCLUSIVE`) listen socket and accepts via the managed `Socket.Accept()` path; the accepted socket is set non-blocking and `TCP_NODELAY`. The accepted file descriptor is handed to a `TlsSocketSession` (`SSL_set_fd`), so OpenSSL reads/writes the fd directly ŌĆö there is no managed intermediate copy for the TLS record layer. - **Handshake.** Owned by the pump: `TryAdvanceHandshake` drives `session.Handshake()`, progressed on `epoll` readiness events until the handshake completes. - **Established I/O.** Once the handshake completes, `ConnectionIoState` drives non-blocking application reads/writes over the same `TlsSocketSession`. ```mermaid flowchart TD L["shared listen socket
EPOLLEXCLUSIVE"] --> W["epoll worker thread
one of WorkerCount"] W --> ACC["accept a connection fd
non-blocking + TCP_NODELAY"] ACC --> BIND["bind fd to a
TlsSocketSession"] BIND --> HS{"handshake
driven on epoll
readiness"} HS -- NeedsTlsContext --> RES["resolve the
TLS context"] RES --> HS HS -- complete --> EST["established connection
ConnectionIoState drives
SSL_read and SSL_write"] EST --> APP["hand DirectTlsConnection
to the Kestrel HTTP layer"] ``` ### Deferred TLS context resolution (SNI + ClientHello) The listener starts every connection on a credential-less **bootstrap `TlsContext`**, so OpenSSL parses the ClientHello and then asks us to resolve the real server context (`NeedsTlsContext`). This is where per-connection selection happens, mid-handshake, synchronously on the pump thread: 1. The connection object (`DirectTlsConnection`, a `ConnectionContext`) is **pre-created** before selection runs, so the selector and callback can see it (for example, `ConnectionId`). 2. `ServerCertificateSelector(connection, sniHost)` runs, falling back to `ServerCertificate` when unset or when it returns null. The chosen certificate resolves a per-certificate `TlsContext` that is **cached** ŌĆö creating one acquires OpenSSL credentials, so repeated SNI hits of the same certificate reuse one native context. ALPN is configured on that context from the endpoint's `HttpProtocols`. 3. `TlsClientHelloBytesCallback(connection, clientHelloBytes)` fires **once**, observe-only, from an `ArrayPool`-rented buffer valid only during the call. It must not block, since it stalls the worker for every connection that worker owns. 4. The resolved context is handed back (`SetContext`) and the handshake continues into key exchange / certificate signing. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant P as epoll pump worker thread participant R as Runtime TlsSocketSession participant A as App DirectTlsEndpointOptions C->>R: ClientHello R->>P: NeedsTlsContext, parsed pre-crypto P->>A: ServerCertificateSelector with connection and SNI host A-->>P: X509Certificate2 P->>A: TlsClientHelloBytesCallback, observe only P->>R: SetContext with per-cert TlsContext and ALPN from HttpProtocols R->>C: ServerHello, Certificate, key exchange R->>P: handshake complete ``` Because the bootstrap context is credential-less, *every* connection defers to `NeedsTlsContext`, so selection and the ClientHello callback fire universally at the earliest possible point. The raw ClientHello is available because the transport runs with `System.Net.Security.CaptureClientHello`. ### Read/write over the TLS session An established connection reads and writes through `ConnectionIoState`, which drives non-blocking `SSL_read` / `SSL_write` over the fd via `TlsSocketSession`. Each call returns a runtime `TlsOperationStatus` that the pump maps **directly** to an `epoll` interest: | Runtime `TlsOperationStatus` | Meaning | Pump action | |---|---|---| | `Complete` | Operation finished; byte count returned | continue | | `NeedMoreData` | Needs more ciphertext from the socket | wait for readable, arm `EPOLLIN` | | `DestinationTooSmall` | Needs to write pending TLS output first | wait for writable, arm `EPOLLOUT` | | `Closed` | Peer closed the connection (EOF) | tear down | The mapping is the same for reads and writes: `NeedMoreData` always means "wait until readable" and `DestinationTooSmall` always means "wait until writable". A renegotiation can flip the direction a read or write is blocked on (a read that must first *write* handshake output, or a write that must first *read* peer records), and this is handled uniformly by re-arming the correct interest ŌĆö the pump switches on the runtime status directly, with no intermediate transport-specific enum. Writes are partial and retry on the unwritten slice. Because DirectTls terminates TLS for every connection, the `TlsSocketSession` is the **only** byte-stream backend ŌĆö the read/write logic is inlined into `ConnectionIoState` with no transport/plaintext seam to switch on. The session owns the `SafeSocketHandle` (`ownsHandle: true`), so dispose closes the fd exactly once. ### Threading model: pump threads and the ThreadPool Two independent mechanisms cooperate per connection, and `DirectTlsConnection` is the object that bridges them: 1. **The epoll pump (a small, fixed set of dedicated threads).** Each of the `WorkerCount` `TlsEventPump` threads owns an `epoll` instance and a set of connection fds. A pump thread blocks in `epoll_wait`; when a fd is ready it runs the TLS syscalls for that connection *on the pump thread* ŌĆö `accept`, handshake, `SSL_read`, `SSL_write` ŌĆö and then signals the connection's per-operation awaitable. A pump thread never runs HTTP or application code; its only job is to move TLS bytes and complete awaitables. 2. **The .NET ThreadPool (where the connection loops, the Kestrel HTTP pipeline, and application code run).** The connection's receive/send loops and everything above them run on ThreadPool threads, exactly like the default transport. `DirectTlsConnection` holds both sides: a `ConnectionIoState` that talks to the pump, and a duplex pipe that talks to Kestrel. Two hand-off points keep the pump thread off the application path: - **Awaitable completion.** `ConnectionIoState.ReadAsync` / `WriteAsync` return a reusable `ValueTask`-style awaitable (`TlsAwaitable`) with `RunContinuationsAsynchronously = true`. When the pump thread finishes an `SSL_read`/`SSL_write`, the awaiting continuation (the receive or send loop) is queued to the **ThreadPool** rather than run inline on the pump thread ŌĆö so a slow loop can never stall the pump thread's other connections. - **Duplex pipe scheduling.** The pipe between the transport and Kestrel is configured with `PipeScheduler.ThreadPool` on the Kestrel-facing side. The receive loop flushes decrypted bytes into `Application.Output`; the pipe dispatches Kestrel's read continuation onto the ThreadPool. Kestrel writes the response into the transport pipe; the send loop drains it and calls back into `ConnectionIoState.WriteAsync`, which re-enters the pump for the actual `SSL_write`. ```mermaid flowchart LR FD["connection fd"] subgraph PUMP["epoll pump thread
one of WorkerCount"] EW["epoll_wait
on owned fds"] IO["ConnectionIoState
SSL_read and SSL_write"] EW --> IO end subgraph TP["ThreadPool"] RL["receive loop"] SL["send loop"] HTTP["Kestrel HTTP pipeline
and application code"] end FD <--> IO IO -- "TlsAwaitable completion
queued, not inline" --> RL RL -- "Application.Output flush" --> PIN["input pipe
PipeScheduler.ThreadPool"] PIN --> HTTP HTTP -- "response bytes" --> POUT["output pipe"] POUT --> SL SL -- "ConnectionIoState
WriteAsync" --> IO ``` The net effect: a small, fixed number of pump threads perform all TLS I/O for many connections with no per-connection thread, while the ThreadPool scales request processing the same way it does for every other transport. Keeping the two apart means application work cannot block TLS I/O for unrelated connections on the same pump, and TLS syscalls do not consume ThreadPool threads. --- ## Connection features `DirectTlsConnection : TransportConnection` *is* its own `IFeatureCollection` (`Features => this`), so the TLS features are implemented **directly on the connection** in a partial file (`DirectTlsConnection.FeatureCollection.cs`) ŌĆö the same pattern `SocketConnection.FeatureCollection.cs` uses. The single source of truth is the `TlsSocketSession`; negotiated values are read live (immutable post-handshake), and the mTLS client certificate is captured at handshake completion. | Feature interface | How DirectTls backs it | Consumer effect | |---|---|---| | `ITlsConnectionFeature` | `ClientCertificate` captured/validated on the pump thread at handshake completion | Its presence makes Kestrel resolve the request scheme as **https** | | `ITlsHandshakeFeature` | `Protocol`, `NegotiatedCipherSuite`, `HostName` read live from the `TlsSocketSession` | `HttpContext` / logging see the negotiated protocol, cipher suite, and SNI host | | `ITlsApplicationProtocolFeature` | negotiated `SslApplicationProtocol` set **directly** on the connection | HTTP/2 selection works with no `UseHttps` middleware bridge ŌĆö the transport references `Kestrel.Core`, so it sets this interface itself | | `IConnectionSocketFeature` | a **non-owning** managed `Socket` reconstructed on demand from the raw fd | parity with the default transport for reading endpoints/socket options | A meaningful improvement over an `SslStream`-bridged design: because this transport references `Kestrel.Core`, it publishes `ITlsApplicationProtocolFeature` itself. ALPN to HTTP/2 works without any `UseHttps` middleware bridging a raw `SslApplicationProtocol` feature. ### `ITlsHandshakeFeature` parity vs `SslStream` Everything the HTTPS layer and apps actually consume is at parity; two omissions are intentional and one obsolete value differs. | Feature / field | SslStream path | DirectTls | Parity | |---|---|---|---| | `https` scheme (`ITlsConnectionFeature` presence) | present | present | yes | | `ClientCertificate` | `SslStream.RemoteCertificate` (live) | captured + validated at handshake completion | yes, handshake-time | | `Protocol` | `SslProtocol` | `Session.NegotiatedProtocol` | yes | | `NegotiatedCipherSuite` | `NegotiatedCipherSuite` | `Session.NegotiatedCipherSuite` | yes | | `HostName` (SNI) | set from the SNI callback | `Session.TargetHostName` | yes | | `Cipher`/`Hash`/`KeyExchange` triples `[Obsolete SYSLIB0058]` | real on TLS 1.2, `None`/`0` on 1.3 | `None`/`0` **always** | matches on 1.3, differs on 1.2 | | ALPN | on `TlsConnectionFeature` | set directly as `ITlsApplicationProtocolFeature` | yes | | `ISslStreamFeature` | returns the `SslStream` | not implemented ŌĆö there is no `SslStream` | intentional omission | The legacy `Cipher`/`Hash`/`KeyExchange` triples are `[Obsolete]`; callers are directed to `NegotiatedCipherSuite`. Populating them would require mapping the negotiated suite back onto the legacy enums; deferred unless a consumer needs it. > `IConnectionSocketFeature`.** DirectTls never owns a managed `Socket`; the accepted fd is handed straight to OpenSSL. To provide this feature for parity we reconstruct a `Socket` from the raw fd, wrapping it in a **non-owning** `SafeSocketHandle` so disposing it does not close the fd the TLS session uses. It is safe only for reading metadata ŌĆö raw `send`/`receive` on it would corrupt the TLS record stream. If nothing on the HTTP path needs it, the cleaner option is to not expose it at all (as we already omit `ISslStreamFeature`). --- ## mTLS (client certificates) Mutual TLS is configured on `DirectTlsEndpointOptions` (`ClientCertificateMode` + `ClientCertificateValidation`): | Mode | Behaviour on DirectTls | |---|---| | `NoCertificate` | No client certificate is requested (default). | | `AllowCertificate` | The server requests a certificate during the **initial handshake**; the connection proceeds whether or not the client presents one. If presented, it is validated and surfaced on `ITlsConnectionFeature.ClientCertificate`. | | `RequireCertificate` | The server requests a certificate during the **initial handshake**; a connection that presents none ŌĆö or one that fails validation ŌĆö is dropped before any request is served. | | `DelayCertificate` | **Not supported ŌĆö throws `NotSupportedException`.** | **How validation runs.** The fd fast path cannot surface the client-certificate verdict the way `SslStream` does, so the transport runs the endpoint's `ClientCertificateValidation` callback **itself**, once, at handshake completion, against a freshly built chain. It records the verdict, drops rejected connections, and exposes an accepted certificate through `ITlsConnectionFeature.ClientCertificate`. **Why `DelayCertificate` is rejected rather than silently degraded.** Post-handshake / on-demand negotiation cannot be resolved deterministically on the fd path: the runtime's socket `Read` API does not surface a "post-handshake auth completed" signal, so a client that *declines* to present a certificate (an empty `Certificate` message, RFC 8446 ┬¦4.4.2) is indistinguishable from "still waiting". Silently treating that as `NoCertificate` would be a security-relevant surprise, so the mode is rejected up front. Will be fixed later

Issues.Title: Fix local WASM Components E2E tests returning 404
Issues.Body:
## Problem

Running the Blazor WebAssembly (client-mode) Components E2E tests locally in Debug (non-trimmed) fails before the app even boots. The browser navigates to ┬Ā`/subdir`, the host returns 404, the Blazor app never loads, and the test fails with:
```
OpenQA.Selenium.BrowserAssertFailedException : Xunit.Sdk.NotEmptyException: Assert.NotEmpty() Failure: Collection was empty
Encountered browser errors
[Severe] chrome-error://chromewebdata/ - Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 404 ()
```

Each affected test also burns its full ~1-minute long-running timeout before failing.

## Why it happened

Introduced in https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/pull/65982.
Locally (non-trimmed), `BlazorWasmTestAppFixture`┬Āhosts `BasicTestApp`┬Āthrough the Blazor Gateway (`BlazorGateway.BuildWebHost`). Two things were misconfigured for that path:

1. Path base never applied. The fixture passed the app's path base as `--pathbase`, but the gateway binds its options from the `Gateway` configuration section (`BlazorGatewayOptions.SectionName = "Gateway"`, read via `Configuration.GetSection("Gateway").Bind(options)`). `--pathbase` populates the top-level `pathbase` key, which nothing reads (so ┬Ā`options.PathBase` stayed null and `app.UsePathBase("/subdir")` was never called).
2. No SPA fallback. The gateway serves assets via ┬ĀMapStaticAssets┬Ā from the endpoints manifest, which has no client-side-routing fallback. So requests under ┬Ā/subdir┬Ā had no ┬Āindex.html┬Ā to fall back to and 404'd.

This only affects local Debug runs. On CI (`TestTrimmedApps == true`) the fixture takes a different code path (`CreateStaticWebHost┬Ā -> ┬ĀStaticSiteStartup`) that already calls `UsePathBase` and `MapFallbackToFile("index.html")`, so CI was unaffected. That's why the breakage was invisible in CI and only bit local development.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix local WASM Components E2E tests returning 404" (#67903)

Issues.Title: Output Caching: MemoryCacheEntryOptions.Size omits retained cache-key bytes from SizeLimit accounting
Issues.Body:
## Summary

The built-in in-process Output Caching store declares each `MemoryCache` entry's size as only the serialized response byte length, while the entry also retains the completed request-derived cache key. As a result, `OutputCacheOptions.SizeLimit` - documented as a byte budget for cache storage - does not account for a retained, variable-size component of every entry. This is a memory-accounting correctness gap, not a demonstrated denial of service.

## What is wrong

* The size-accounting invariant is incomplete: the declared entry size should approximate the retained variable content of the entry, but it counts only the serialized response value and omits the retained key.
* `MemoryOutputCacheStore` sets `MemoryCacheEntryOptions.Size = value.Length`, where `value` is the serialized response. The completed UTF-16 string key is retained by the backing `MemoryCache` for the lifetime of the entry but contributes nothing to the configured byte budget.
* Because keys vary by URL components by default (method, scheme, host, path, and all query keys/values), entries with small responses and large keys under-report their actual retained footprint most.

## Why it matters (defense in depth)

* Correctness: `OutputCacheOptions.SizeLimit` is presented in bytes and treated by operators as the cache-storage bound. Omitting a retained, variable-size portion of each entry means the tracked size can materially understate the retained key content, so the configured budget is a weaker estimate than intended. This is most visible on memory-constrained hosts (containers, small plans) where `SizeLimit` was sized assuming it reflects real cache content.
* Hardening: charging retained key content strengthens the accounting boundary the option is meant to enforce and reduces the amount of key material that can be retained beyond the configured estimate. It does not claim to be an exact managed-heap ceiling; `MemoryCache` sizes are caller-defined estimates and the runtime does not measure object graphs or trim under memory pressure.

## Affected code

* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/Memory/MemoryOutputCacheStore.cs:104-132 - sets `Size = value.Length`; the retained key is not charged
* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:20-40 - wires `OutputCacheOptions.SizeLimit` into the backing `MemoryCache` `SizeLimit`
* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheOptions.cs:8-33 - documents `SizeLimit` in bytes as the cache-storage bound
* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheKeyProvider.cs:13-330 - materializes the completed string key that the store retains
* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/CacheEntryHelpers.cs:8-53 - existing repository convention: string content estimated as `Length * sizeof(char)`, body as byte length

## Recommended fix

* Selected approach: at the built-in memory-store sink, include the completed key's UTF-16 content in the declared entry size using overflow-safe `long` arithmetic:

  ```csharp
  Size = checked(value.LongLength + ((long)key.Length * sizeof(char)))
  ```

  This is computed where both the finalized key and the serialized value are known, so it covers every key producer (default policy plus configured header/route/query/prefix/custom variation) without changing key identity. It matches the existing repository estimation convention (`string.Length * sizeof(char)` for characters, byte length for buffers). It intentionally excludes object headers, dictionary/`CacheEntry` structures, callbacks, allocator overhead, and tag-index metadata; those are workload- and runtime-dependent and outside the option's existing byte-content model.

* Alternatives considered:
  * Charge a UTF-8/serialized key representation ŌĆö rejected: does not match the retained in-memory UTF-16 representation and adds a scan/encoding cost.
  * Hash or truncate keys ŌĆö rejected/prohibited: truncation creates response-selection collisions; hashing changes shipped key identity, diagnostics, and distributed/custom-store semantics.
  * Separate per-key length limit ŌĆö deferred as optional defense in depth: bounds one key but does not correct aggregate accounting; would require API review if public.
  * Add a separate aggregate key budget ŌĆö rejected: duplicates `MemoryCache` admission, replacement, concurrency, and eviction state.
  * Documentation-only ŌĆö rejected: leaves an obvious retained variable component outside the byte estimate.

* Compatibility, migration, and versioning:
  * No public API change and no `PublicAPI` baseline change.
  * Behavior change: with the same `SizeLimit`, applications with long or high-cardinality keys fit fewer entries and may evict/compact earlier. Requests still succeed uncached when an entry is not admitted. Worth a release-note line.
  * In-process entries do not survive restart, so no migration or dual-read path is needed.
  * Redis and custom `IOutputCacheStore` implementations are unaffected; `OutputCacheOptions.SizeLimit` already applies only to the built-in memory store.
  * Do not increase the default 100 MB limit to compensate; that would partially hide the corrected accounting.

## Acceptance criteria

* [ ] The declared entry size includes the retained key's UTF-16 content in addition to the serialized response length.
* [ ] An entry whose value-plus-key size exceeds `SizeLimit` is not retained, and the originating request still completes successfully (uncached).
* [ ] Accounting is charged by UTF-16 code units (verified for BMP and surrogate-pair keys), not UTF-8 bytes or scalar count.
* [ ] Multiple distinct entries consume aggregate capacity by value-plus-key size; an empty value still charges the key.
* [ ] Equal-key replacement accounts for one logical entry; an oversized replacement removes the prior entry.
* [ ] Tagged entries follow the same size rule and rejected tagged candidates leave no stale tag-index state after callback completion.
* [ ] Size arithmetic is overflow-safe (checked/`long`).
* [ ] Behavior change (fewer entries at a given `SizeLimit` for large keys) is documented in release notes.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Output Caching: MemoryCacheEntryOptions.Size omits retained cache-key bytes from SizeLimit accounting" (#67911)

Issues.Title: Hardening: make Response/Output cache base-key serialization injective across PathBase and Path
Issues.Body:
## Summary

The base cache-key serialization in ASP.NET Core Response Caching and Output Caching combines `HttpRequest.PathBase` and `HttpRequest.Path` without a boundary marker. The resulting key is not an injective function of the `(PathBase, Path)` pair, so two distinct component tuples can serialize to the same key.

## What is wrong

* Both key providers append the normalized `PathBase` value immediately followed by the normalized `Path` value with no delimiter between the two fields. Every other structured field in the key is separated by the reserved record delimiter, but this one boundary is not.
* Because there is no separator, the mapping from the structured pair `(PathBase, Path)` to the key string is not injective: distinct pairs whose concatenations are equal produce identical keys.
* The providers already reject the reserved delimiter characters from both path components, so the boundary can be represented unambiguously, but currently is not.
* Origin components:
  * Response Caching base-key construction.
  * Output Caching base-key construction.

## Why it matters (defense in depth)

* Correctness: a cache key should be a faithful, reversible encoding of the request identity fields it claims to represent. A non-injective field boundary is a latent correctness defect independent of any attacker.
* Hardening: preserving the `PathBase`/`Path` boundary keeps cache entries isolated when the decomposition is meaningful, and it strengthens isolation across compositions that populate `PathBase` (for example forwarded-prefix handling, `UsePathBase`, `Map`, IIS sub-application paths, and HTTP.sys URL prefixes) as well as shared distributed stores. This reduces reliance on downstream configuration to prevent accidental cross-entry aliasing.
* This is filed as a normal engineering hardening item, not a security advisory. No supported, correctly configured deployment has been shown to make the two tuples externally selectable, so the value here is correctness and defense in depth.

## Affected code

* src/Middleware/ResponseCaching/src/ResponseCachingKeyProvider.cs:36-72 - `CreateBaseKey` appends `PathBase` and `Path` consecutively in both the case-sensitive and default branches.
* src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheKeyProvider.cs:93-133 - `TryAppendBaseKey` repeats the same boundary-free composition.

## Recommended fix

Selected approach: emit the existing reserved key delimiter (`KeyDelimiter`, `\x1e`) unconditionally between the normalized `PathBase` and `Path` fields in both providers, in both the case-sensitive and case-insensitive branches. The delimiter is already forbidden inside both components, so this makes the encoding injective with no escaping, decoder, or parsing logic. Insert it unconditionally (including when `PathBase` is empty) so patched instances never read an ambiguous legacy key.

Alternatives considered:

* Conditional delimiter only when `PathBase` is non-empty. Rejected: during a rolling deployment against a shared store, a patched instance with an empty `PathBase` could still read a key written by an unpatched instance, leaving the two formats partially aliased.
* Length-prefix or fixed-width length encoding of each component. Sound but unnecessary while the reserved delimiter is already rejected from the inputs; it adds format complexity (and a permanent code-unit-versus-byte decision) with no additional benefit here.
* Fully structural or tagged binary key format. Deferred: this is a larger change to two mature hot paths and is only warranted as a broader key-schema revision.
* Escaping or URI-encoding the components. Rejected: canonical, prefix-free escaping is harder to maintain, and `PathString` has its own handling of preserved encoded separators that could introduce new aliases.

Compatibility, migration, and versioning:

* No public API change; `IOutputCacheStore` and the response-cache store contract already treat keys as opaque strings.
* All keys change once. In-process stores (the default for both middlewares) flush naturally on process restart, so there is no migration concern there.
* For shared or persistent Output Cache stores (for example Redis or a custom `IOutputCacheStore`), the new keys occupy a namespace disjoint from legacy keys. Do not add a fallback read of the old key format, as that would reintroduce the aliasing. Expect a one-time cold-cache transition and legacy entries expiring under their existing TTL.

## Acceptance criteria

* [ ] For both providers, distinct `(PathBase, Path)` pairs whose concatenations are equal produce distinct base keys.
* [ ] The property holds with `UseCaseSensitivePaths` both enabled and disabled.
* [ ] An empty `PathBase` combined with a full `Path` produces a different key than a full `PathBase` combined with an empty `Path` for the same combined text.
* [ ] Equal pairs remain deterministic, and existing delimiter-rejection behavior for both components is unchanged.
* [ ] Output Cache host suppression, key prefix, and vary dimensions continue to function and do not remove the new boundary.
* [ ] Unit tests in the Response Caching and Output Caching key-provider test suites lock the injectivity property.
* [ ] Compatibility note for shared/persistent Output Cache stores is documented (one-time key-format change, no legacy fallback read).
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Hardening: make Response/Output cache base-key serialization injective across PathBase and Path" (#67907)

## Summary

Issues.Title: Enable prepend/append detection with the default `ItemComparer` in `Virtualize<TItem>`
Issues.Body:
# Summary

`Virtualize<TItem>` can keep the viewport anchored when items are prepended/appended by detecting how the loaded window shifted between renders. Previously, for a custom `ItemsProvider`, this detection only ran when the consumer explicitly assigned `ItemComparer`. This PR removes that gate so detection also works with the default comparer (`EqualityComparer<TItem>.Default`), which covers value-type items and reference-type items where the provider returns stable references.

## The problem

Prepend/append detection was gated behind an internal `_itemComparerExplicitlySet` flag. Because `ItemComparer` defaults to a valid comparer (`EqualityComparer<TItem>.Default`), there was no technical reason to require an explicit assignment - the gate just disabled a working code path:

ŌĆó Value-type items (e.g. ┬Ārecord, struct, int) compare correctly by value with the default comparer, yet got no prepend detection unless the user redundantly assigned a comparer.
ŌĆó Reference-type items with stable references compare correctly by reference, but were likewise excluded.

The net effect: viewports jumped on prepend for perfectly comparable items, unless the consumer knew to set `ItemComparer` explicitly.

## The fix

Remove `_itemComparerExplicitlySet` and its guard. If the comparer is not provided, the default one is used.
Tests for default comparer that will check if there's an improvement for users who don't change their Virtualize settings when upgrading from net10 to net11. (In net10 the tests added would fail)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Enable prepend/append detection with the default `ItemComparer` in `Virtualize<TItem>`" (#67905)

# Adding analyzer to warn about JSInterop calls not wrapped in a try catch block

- [x] You've read the [Contributor Guide](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CODE-OF-CONDUCT.md).

## Description
Added analyzer that shows warnning and code fixer that wraps the JSInterop calls (InvokeAsync, InvokeVoidAsync etc.) in try/catch block.

Also added some tests:

| Area | Test Scenario | Expected Result |
|------|---------------|-----------------|
| Analyzer | Unguarded `IJSRuntime` extension call (`InvokeVoidAsync`) | Warning is reported |
| Analyzer | Unguarded `IJSObjectReference` extension call (`InvokeVoidAsync`) | Warning is reported |

| Analyzer | Unguarded `IJSObjectReference.InvokeAsync<T>` call | Warning is reported |
| Analyzer | Unguarded `IJSInProcessRuntime.Invoke<T>` call | Warning is reported |
| Analyzer | Unguarded `IJSInProcessObjectReference.Invoke<T>` call | Warning is reported |
| Analyzer | JS interop call already inside `try/catch` | No diagnostic is reported |
| Analyzer | JS interop call nested in guarded flow (`if` inside `try/catch`) | No diagnostic is reported |

| Code Fixer | Unguarded awaited `InvokeAsync<T>` | Call is wrapped in `try/catch` |
| Code Fixer | Unguarded non-awaited `InvokeVoidAsync` | Call is wrapped in `try/catch` |


Fixes #67306

Issues.Title: The non-scaffolded razor page "Register" does not recognise Password.RequiredLength.
Issues.Body:
### Describe the bug
Setting password length through 
```
public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services){
  ...
  services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
  {
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
  });
  ...
```
does not change the non-scaffolded razor page as it always says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long." if inputed password is < 6 characters.

### To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a new ASP.NET Core Web 2.1 application
2. Select "Web application (Model-View Controller)" 
3. Select "Change authentication"
3.1. Select "Individual User Account"
4. Select Ok to have the project scaffolded.
5. Open file Startup.cs
6. Add 
```
services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
{
  options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
});
```
to method `ConfigureServices`.
7. Press F5 (or select your favourite start-debug method).
8. In the web browser that pops up
8.1. Select Register
8.2. Type "a@example.com" as user name (or select your favourite fast-to-type email addresss).
8.3. Type "a" as password (or any password shorter than 6 characters).
8.4. Select Register

### Expected behavior
The web page should have been updated with information about successful creation of user.

### Actual behaviour
The web page says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long."

### Additional context
I have a question at stack overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53620708/how-do-i-set-password-options-in-aspnet-core-2-1 that explains the same issue if this is not clear enough.

This issue might be duplicate of #774.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "The non-scaffolded razor page "Register" does not recognise Password.RequiredLength." (#2091)

Issues.Title: Remember me isPersistent understanding
Issues.Body:
This is more of a question but its not enough of a question that i could post it up on stack overflow without risking it just getting closed as primary opinion based or to broad.   So i am going to put it here in hopes of getting an answer.

The issue is the understanding of how remember me is supposed to work.    My problem is that its not doing what we would expect it to do.   

Management expects when a user logs in and clicks remember me that they will then not be required to type their login and password again for a month.  Whether or not they will still have to type in the 2fa code is up for debate.

What is actually happening is that i can close the browser reboot and come back for about an hour and 15 minutes and then i will be forced to type my password again.    This still happens even with a valid `.AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie set that is not due to expire for another month.

After digging around in the code i found that Remember me is actually isPersistent and it is defined as.

>Flag indicating whether the sign-in cookie should persist after the browser is closed.

Which brings you to:

There are two different types of Cookies:

- session cookies, held in memory, and which expire once the browser exits
- persistent cookies, which have a time-to-live, are persisted on disk, and are sent by the browser until their time-to-live has elapsed.

All this being said remember machine 2fa works as expected i am not prompted again for the 2fa and i have a cookie on my machine `Identity.TwoFactorRememberMe`.

So I guess my question is why are we not getting a `Identity.FactorRememberMe` or why isn't sign in respecting a long life time of the `AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie?

How is this supposed to work exactly?   I am following the code here even though we have normal MVC and not razor pages like you have in the example.  I am still just calling 

>var signInUserResult = await _signInManager.PasswordSignInAsync(userName, password, rememberMe, true);

Note: I am using Asp .net Identity in Identity server 4 application.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Remember me isPersistent understanding" (#2079)

Issues.Body:
I'm using ASPNET Core 2.1 with Razor Pages UI Library and noticed that whilst registered social logins appear when I open the Login page, they disappear after an error like incorrect password.

There seems to be a bug in Identity\Pages\Account\Login.cshtml.cs as 
````
 public IList<AuthenticationScheme> ExternalLogins { get; set; }

````
is only initialised in OnGetAsync(), but if an error happens OnPostAsync() then Page() is returned so OnGetAsyn() doesn't get called. Changing error handling in  OnPostAsync() so RedirectToPage() is returned seems to fix the problem.

Please confirm this is a bug and comment on my fix

Issues.Title: Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?
Issues.Body:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.identity.entityframeworkcore.identityuser?view=aspnetcore-1.1

I'm looking through various implementations from the [readme](https://github.com/aspnet/Identity/blob/master/README.md) and I've noticed they all use `IdentityUser ` for their concrete type to pass to `UserManager`, or have had to craft a custom type to be able to have a contract between the database and the .NET AspNetIdentity implentation they've written (see the DynamoDB library).

I've had to do this too for the [Marten implementation I've written](https://github.com/yetanotherchris/Marten.AspNetIdentity), and the reason is, a `TUser` that your `UserStore` takes, should really be an interface - it will always have an email, most likely an Id of some sort, firstname, lastname and then a bag of properties.

Things become even more abstract when dealing with claims.

If everyone is using `IdentityUser` or their own custom `TUser` doesn't this defeat the purpose of AspnetIdentity - which is to be able to switch and change implementations easily? That isn't possible if your consumer is tightly coupled with a `TUser` implementation of the library.

### Example `TUser` implementations

- [MongoIdentityUser](https://github.com/tugberkugurlu/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/MongoIdentityUser.cs)
- [DynamoIdentityUser](https://github.com/miltador/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/DynamoIdentityUser.cs)
- [Redis uses `IdentityUser<string>`](https://github.com/aguacongas/Identity.Redis/blob/develop/src/Aguacongas.Identity.Redis/UserStore.cs)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?" (#2056)

Issues.Title: [Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?
Issues.Body:
My security team escalated to me that the `PasswordHasher` is vulnerable to heap inspection, as the password is of type `String`. 
It should be instead a byte array / span of bytes cleared at the end of the process, as it is done by any cryptographic operation in corefx. CC @bartonjs.
Same problem with any password related types (`IPasswordValidator`, and maybe also the `IUserPasswordStore`)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "[Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?" (#2051)

Issues.Title: Cannot start a webapi on specific SSL port managed by IISExpress
Issues.Body:
A brand new netcore webapi 2.2 is created. 

Current launch settings:
```
{
  "$schema": "http://json.schemastore.org/launchsettings.json",
  "iisSettings": {
    "windowsAuthentication": false,
    "anonymousAuthentication": true,
    "iisExpress": {
      "applicationUrl": "http://localhost:62813",
      "sslPort": 44396
    }
  },
```

After setting the SSL port to anything such as 11000, application won't get started on IIS Express, but the same app runs correctly on port 11000 after being published to local IIS (https://localhost:11000).

The only way to start it on IIS Express is to keep 443 prefix (i.e. 44300, 44301, etc). 

Is this restriction by design as stated [here](https://developercommunity.visualstudio.com/content/problem/39430/changing-port-number-in-a-web-project-does-not-imm.html?childToView=43139#comment-43139)?

"You can change that value to whatever you want." definitely is not working as stated here: https://github.com/aspnet/Tooling/issues/286

Issues.Title: System.Collections.Immutable dll being replace by Microsoft.Net.Compilers
Issues.Body:
I have referenced `system.collections.immutable.dll` from the nuget package and I also have the nuget package installed for `Microsoft.Net.Compilers`. Everything works fine in my local but when I push my code to bamboo, in the runtime I get `Could not load file or assembly 'System.Collections.Immutable, Version=1.2.3.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' or one of its dependencies. The located assembly's manifest definition does not match the assembly reference. (Exception from HRESULT: 0x80131040)`

When I checked logs on bamboo when it generates the artifacts, I can see that initially it copied the correct dll here:

`build    04-Apr-2019 03:20:33      Copying file from "E:\agt01\ENS-EB580-JOB1\Bin\Packages\System.Collections.Immutable.1.5.0\lib\netstandard1.0\System.Collections.Immutable.dll" to "E:\agt01\ENS-EB580-JOB1\Binaries\Release\\temp\Web\_PublishedWebsites\MyService\bin\System.Collections.Immutable.dll"`.

but then at some point, it replaced this dll with this: `build    04-Apr-2019 03:20:33      Copying file from "E:\agt01\ENS-EB580-JOB1\Bin\Packages\Microsoft.Net.Compilers.1.0.0\tools\System.Collections.Immutable.dll" to "E:\agt01\ENS-EB580-JOB1\Binaries\Release\\temp\Web\_PublishedWebsites\MyService\bin\System.Collections.Immutable.dll".`

How can I make sure that bamboo does not replace this dll and just uses it from the nuget package installed?

Issues.Title: Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019
Issues.Body:
Apologize if my feedback may sound controversial or may spark a debate.

I need to be sure that the problem mentioned on this [page](https://github.com/aspnet/Tooling/blob/master/known-issues-vs2017.md#to-create-aspnet-core-10--11-projects-install-visual-studios-net-core-10---11-developer-tools-for-web-component) about .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling is installed when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project should not happen or should not be supported in VS 2019.

These are my main concerns:

1. VS 2019 is about to be released in around early 2019
2. ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 is not going to be supported anymore, based on [this page](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/microsoft-support.md).
3. Creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 will always install the .NET Core 1.0/1.1 web tooling, and this is not necessary when previous reason has reached the unsupported period and it's quite near or might be the same time of VS 2019 release.
4. Based on the problem mentioned above, VS 2017 has no "built in" tooling when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project, but the external tooling may help. Therefore for those that still need to maintain ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 projects, we could use VS 2017 as long as the VS 2017 is still in support lifecycle or in mainstream support lifecycle.

I welcome feedback ­¤Öé
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019" (#1078)

Issues.Title: Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json
Issues.Body:
I used to be able to use `dotnet watch` in Visual Studio by adding the following section to `launchSettings.json` and starting the project without debugging using CTRL + F5.

```
    "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    },
```

This used to work in ASP.NET Core 1.1 but no longer does. According to https://github.com/aspnet/DotNetTools/issues/404 the new format for ASP.NET Core 2.0 is:

```
   "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "workingDirectory": "C:\\Users\\namc\\source\\repos\\WebApplication2\\WebApplication2",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    }
```

The problem with this is the hard coded `executablePath` which is Windows specific and the `workingDirectory` which is specific to wherever I did a Git clone on my local machine.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json" (#1067)

Issues.Title: Nesting bug
Issues.Body:
The is a problem nesting embedded resources.  I was experimenting with nesting .cshtml files using the existing rules and did this:

Modal.cs (bootstrap modal web component)
   -> Modal.cs.cshtml (it's view file)

`Modal.cs.cshtml` is set to Embedded Resource, and clearly shows it, but is completed IGNORED by the build process (simply open the DLL in a text editor [such as Notepad++] and scroll to the bottom and look for text).  If I rename the file to "Modal.cshtml" instead it works perfectly fine!  :/  I don't think whether or not a file is embedded should have and role in excluding a file from being embedded so this seems like a bug to me.

Yes, I'm aware of upcoming nesting controls in v15.6, but I'm not able to install it yet, and regardless, this should work fine, so it should be looked into nonetheless.

Just to add: I'm using wild-cards to include the file.  If I explicitly add it I get this:

`Duplicate 'EmbeddedResource' items were included. ...`

So it's clearly found.  It's obviously something up with the build process not finding the nested file.

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: `OpenApiVersion` default change from `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1` to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`
Issues.Body:
### Description

The default OpenApi spec version is changing to 3.2 instead of 3.1.

### Version

.NET 11 Preview 6

### Previous behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaulted to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1`

### New behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaults to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [ ] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We aim to always use the latest version of the spec by default.

### Recommended action

Generally, no action is needed except if users encounter problems, like other toolings that don't support OpenApi 3.2.

If you're impacted, you can always configure the OpenApiOptions to use 3.1:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenApi(options =>
{
    options.OpenApiVersion = OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1;
});
```

### Affected APIs

- `OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: `OpenApiVersion` default change from `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1` to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`" (#530)

Issues.Title: ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027
Issues.Body:
# ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027

> Update: The ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support date was updated from April 7, 2027 to April 13, 2027 to align with the Microsoft servicing release cycles.

Today we are announcing that ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach **end of support on April 13, 2027**. After that date, Microsoft will no longer provide security patches, bug fixes, or technical support for ASP.NET Core 2.3.

This announcement provides the required 12 months of advance notice, as defined by the [Microsoft Support Lifecycle Policy](https://learn.microsoft.com/lifecycle/faq/general-lifecycle#what-is-the-lifecycle-policy-for-tools-) for products classified as "Tools."

## Current support policy

ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages (latest patched version only) are currently [supported](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet#dotnet-core) on .NET Framework, following the support cycle for those .NET Framework versions. After April 13, 2027, this support will end regardless of the .NET Framework version in use.

Note that the [ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/support/policy/aspnet/2.3-packages) include the Entity Framework 2.3 packages, which will end support on the same date.

## Impact of end of support

After ASP.NET Core 2.3 reaches end of support:

- No new security updates will be issued for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- Continuing to use an unsupported version may expose your applications to security vulnerabilities.
- Technical support will no longer be available for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- The ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages will be deprecated.

## Upgrade recommendation

We recommend upgrading to a [currently supported version of .NET](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet), such as .NET 10 (LTS).

To help with the upgrade process, we recommend using [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview), which provides AI-powered assistance to help you analyze, plan, and execute your migration to a modern .NET version.

## Resources

- [ASP.NET Core Support Policy](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet)
- [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview)
- [Migrate from ASP.NET to ASP.NET Core](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/proper-to-2x/)
- [Blog post: ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet-core-2-3-end-of-support/)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027" (#529)

Issues.Title: Add automation to create docs tracking issues for breaking changes
Issues.Body:
Automates creation of documentation tracking issues in `dotnet/AspNetCore.Docs` when breaking change issues are opened or labeled in this repository.

## Implementation

**New workflow:** `.github/workflows/create-docs-issue.yaml`

- Triggers on `issues.opened` and `issues.labeled` events with "Breaking change" label
- Creates issue in docs repo with:
  - Title: `[Breaking Change] <original title>`
  - Body: Link to source issue, full context
  - Labels: `breaking-change`, `aspnet/Announcements`
- Posts comment on source issue with link to docs issue

## Duplicate Prevention

Multi-layer detection to handle race conditions and partial failures:
- Checks for existing bot comments in source issue
- Searches docs repo by title before creating
- Concurrency control keyed on issue number

## Error Handling

Separate try-catch blocks for each operation:
- Comment listing failures ŌåÆ proceed with search
- Search failures ŌåÆ proceed with creation (fail open to avoid missing docs)
- Comment posting failures ŌåÆ don't fail workflow (docs issue already created)
- Permission errors ŌåÆ explicit message about `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` requirement

## Setup Required

Configure `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` repository secret with `repo` scope for cross-repository issue creation. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` but will fail with clear error message.

## Security

Explicit minimal permissions: `issues: write`, `contents: read`

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Deprecating WebHostBuilder,  IWebHost, and WebHost
Issues.Body:
### Description

`WebHostBuilder` was replaced by `HostBuilder` ([generic host](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder)) in 3.0 and we also added [`WebApplicationBuilder`](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) in 6.0.

We are now marking `WebHostBuilder` as obsolete to encourage users to move on to the alternatives which is where future investments will occur, and we're also marking `IWebHost` and `WebHost` as deprecated to clean up other parts of the now obsolete web host story.

### Version

.NET 10 RC 1

### Previous behavior

```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

### New behavior

Using `WebHostBuilder` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR004**:
> warning ASPDEPR004: WebHostBuilder is deprecated in favor of HostBuilder and WebApplicationBuilder. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/004.

Using `IWebHost` or `WebHost` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR008**:
> warning ASPDEPR008: WebHost is obsolete. Use HostBuilder or WebApplicationBuilder instead. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/008.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We recommend the use of `HostBuilder` and `WebApplication` going forward over `WebHostBuilder` because they have all the features of `WebHostBuilder` and will be the focus of future investment.

### Recommended action

See https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/host/generic-host or https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/minimal-apis/webapplication for more details on the different hosting models. As well as [HostBuilder replaces WebHostBuilder](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder) and [Introducing WebApplication](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) for migration guides.

Below is an example of converting `WebHostBuilder` to `HostBuilder`:

**Before**
```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

**After**
```csharp
using var host = new HostBuilder()
    .ConfigureWebHost(webHostBuilder =>
    {
        webHostBuilder
            .UseTestServer() // If using TestServer
            .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
            .UseStartup()
            .UseKestrel();
    })
    .Build();
await host.StartAsync();

var testServer = host.GetTestServer();
```

Both Copilot and ChatGpt have given good results so far when asked to convert a WebHostBuilder example to use HostBuilder. Below is an example prompt:
<details>
<summary>Example AI Prompt</summary>

> Can you convert
>
> var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
>     .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
>     .UseStartup()
>     .UseKestrel();
> // Test code might use TestServer:
> var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
>
> to use HostBuilder.

</details>

### Affected APIs
